Title: Ciência viva no laboratório (STEM, with a few human science internships)
Category: Biomed, Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Robotics, Human sciences, Finance
Description:
- short term summer internships in university labs, mainly bio related BUT ones that involve robotics, chemistry, compsci, physics are rarer and so the first ones to run out of spots
- There are a few internships catered towards humanities students, however, they host more students at once (ie. 10-20)
- internships occur during the summer, official application date will be on the website around early April, browse through early on and plan ahead for highest chances of getting desired internship.
- For non-Portuguese speakers, most labs will be able to host you, but there are a few with their descriptions written in English, those would be the most suitable for you
- Some will provide accommodation and/or lunch for free
HowToApply: Sign into the website, choose your top 4 internships, then fill out the application forms. You’ll be admitted to one of them. A short paragraph describing your motivation to participate in that specific internship is needed.

Title: internships: Champalimaud Foundation
Description: You can apply to be a short-term summer intern at a fantastic research institution! The Champalimaud Foundation has research groups focused on the most cutting edge research on neuroscience and cancer, in Portugal.
HowToApply: Applications are quite competitive, though they are open year-round. You will need to apply half a year in advance. Browse through the Lab’s webpage carefully and check their requirements (ie. Grade transcripts), some may only be applied to via a form through their office of research internships. Get to know the work of the lab you are applying to, and make sure you are genuinely interested! Email the Lab’s principal investigator, or, if specified on their webpage, you can email a postgraduate/graduate researcher.

Title: National competition for young scientists ( Concurso nacional jovens cientistas)
Category: Biology, Earth sciences, environmental science, Health sciences, Social Economic sciences, Engineering, Technology, Physics, Mathematics, Chemistry
Description: Challenge your overall skillset as a young scientist by creating your own project! Participate in a project in an area of your choice by writing up a scientific report for submission. You may wish to get sponsored from outside institutions(ie. University laboratories) for better access to resources.
HowToApply: Since submissions end in late April, you should start planning in September to reduce stress and ensure everything runs smoothly. IB1s should participate with very careful planning as it will increase your workload.

Title: Hospital internships
Description: Get insight into what it’s like to work at a hospital, in order to see if you’re interested in this area. Shadowing a doctor can be a valuable way for you to experience the realities of the medical profession. Hospital de Luz is known for having accepted high school interns in the past.
HowToApply: Contact your local Luz Hospital or any hospital through email/phone and ask to intern in administration, with shadowing doctors as part of your internship. (don’t call the emergency line for patients; look through their official website).

Title: Animal shelter volunteer
AgeGroup: CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8, IG1, IG2, IB1, IB2
Description: Some vet university courses require a certain number of work experience hours. Contact your local animal shelter to see if volunteering positions are available. You’ll gain real life experience on what its like to take care of animals. There's a possibility of bringing lice/fleas or animal-transmitted diseases back to your home, please ensure you are fully sanitised after working.
HowToApply: Best to plan around 3 months in advance. See the animal shelter’s webpage, some you can contact directly through email, others may require you to go through a training phase.

Title: Veterinary internships
Description: Some vet university courses require a certain number of work experience hours. Contact your local veterinary hospital to see if internship positions are available. This is a great way to find out if being a veterinarian would be a good career path for you.
HowToApply: Best to plan around 4 months in advance, email several veterinary hospitals near you.

Title: Service civil international(17 and above)
Description: These are called workcamps, but are actually volunteering programs that last from 1-3 weeks. Pick a program from their official website, then apply according to the specifics on another website that is local to the Portuguese branch.
HowToApply: Apply following open positions and start date. Plan 6 months in advance as you might need to apply for a visa, then fly to another country.

Title: Volunteering in Portugal
AgeGroup: CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8, IG1, IG2, IB1, IB2
Description: Apart from being a great person, universities like to see character from their applicants. Volunteering shows that you care about your community, or that you’re passionate about a specific cause.
HowToApply: Check for programs near you or international programs. Application varies, but it’s recommended that you plan this in advance.

Title: Volunteering overseas
Description: Paid volunteering ranges from animal shelters, teaching English, to child care and construction projects. There are many possibilities; plan ahead and choose what you think is within your capabilities. Paid volunteering ensures a safer, more structured experience, especially for high schoolers under 18 or those who are travelling solo for the first time. Most paid volunteering also ensures that food and accommodation, pickups from airports are taken care of. These can be expensive, and you need to be careful in finding a trustworthy volunteering project. Some may feel like a travel/tour agency, but it’s advisable for first-time volunteers not to pick the most labour/planning-intensive projects that could potentially overwhelm you.
HowToApply: You may need to plan half a year or at least 4 months in advance if deciding to volunteer in countries where visas and vaccinations are required.
Link1: Recommended companies(there are many out there): VidaEdu, Volunteer HQ

Title: ChemRus
AgeGroup: CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8, IG1, IG2, IB1, IB2
Description: A chemistry competition that involves making a video about a chemical process of your choosing. You can watch winning videos from past years to get a sense of what they’re looking for. Details and regulations can be found on the official website. Students should participate in groups of 3-5, supervised by a professor.
HowToApply: Submit your video around January – beginning of April

Title: Local math competitions
AgeGroup: CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8, IG1, IG2, IB1, IB2
Description: Challenge your mathematical skills and make yourself stand out! Contact your math teacher to see if the school has plans to enrol students in these competitions; otherwise, you may need to sign up as an individual contestant. Some may be in Portuguese; non-PT speakers won't be able to participate. If you decide to participate, you may need to brush up on math terminology in Portuguese.
HowToApply: See application details on official websites.

Title: Youth parliament
Description: An educational programme aiming to promote debate on pressing issues, between young people in Europe. After completing your first experience as a delegate, you may wish to apply to positions such as a Chairperson, Organiser, Journalist, etc.
HowToApply: Fill out an application that will be posted on each session’s Facebook Page, or contact the person responsible for managing members. francisco.moura@eyp.pt

Title: MUN (Model United Nations)
AgeGroup: CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8, IG1, IG2, IB1, IB2
Description: Model United Nations is a club where you pretend to be a representative from a country and talk about important world problems. learn how countries work together through researching a particular issue, then discuss and debate your chosen country's ideas with representatives of other countries. The solutions you agree upon are compiled in a document called a resolution. MUN is a great way to improve your speaking, thinking, and teamwork skills while learning about different countries and global issues. Students who are interested in politics, history, and law-related professions should definitely try it out!
HowToApply:
Mun conferences are hosted by other schools, typically international schools(including schools in Portugal, and in other countries). Attending these conferences usually requires an entrance fee, which used to be covered by our school's budget. Speak to a teacher who may be able to communicate with the school that is hosting a conference, as it is likely they will require an adult superviser to accompany you. Enrollment occurs 6-8 months before the actual conference. 
Note: many Mun conferences are being hosted in the world every year, some of them WILL ACCEPT individual participants. Make sure to do your research on past related conferences beforehand to ensure a good experience.

Title: Local Hackathons
Description: If you're interested in pursuing a career in engineering, software development, or even cybersecurity, hackathons are a fantastic for challenge your coding skills! You can meet like-minded people at hackathons, in addition to significantly developing your knowledge in computer science.
HowToApply: Applications may vary for each event; be sure to check if they're individual or team events. Plan around 3-4 months to reduce stress and ensure proper preparation.

Title: Free online courses
Category: Biomed, Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Robotics, Human sciences, Finance, Computer science,  Veterinary sciences, Human sciences, Mathematics, Earth sciences, environmental science, Health sciences, Social Economic sciences, Engineering, Technology, Mathematics, Chemistry
AgeGroup: CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8, IG1, IG2, IB1, IB2
Description: Not sure if you're interested in a particular area? Start a free online course to see if it's right for you! There is a wide range of completely free courses online, published by accredited universities, free for you to explore.

Title: Bow seat ocean awareness contest
AgeGroup: CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8, IG1, IG2, IB1, IB2
Description: Submit a creation of your own inspired by prompts provided by the contest, ranging from digital art, paintings, to poetry. Focusing on reflections about the climate issues our planet currently faces, this is an opportunity to alert the world on the urgency of these severe issues. Showcase your artistic skills while being an environmentally conscious global citizen!
HowToApply: Register an account on the website, ensuring that you have an adult supervisor (parent, guardian, art teacher, etc.) You are allowed to submit one piece in one category of your choosing, and make sure to take pictures of your creative process to evidence the authenticity of your piece. Submissions end in early June, you should plan at least 2 months in advance.

Title: John Locke essay contest
Category: Philosophy, Theology, History, Politics, Law, Economics
Description: The John Locke Institute Essay Competition encourages students to develop independent thinking, critical analysis, and persuasive writing by tackling thought-provoking questions beyond the school curriculum. Essays are judged by senior academics from top universities like Oxford and Princeton, with prizes awarded across seven subject categories and a Grand Prize for the overall best entry. A separate prize is also given for the best essay in the under-15 junior category.
HowToApply: Choose a prompt from the 7 areas provided by their website, then write a 2000-word essay complete with citations and authorship declarations. Registrations can be made on the website via creating your own account; these open early april and end in late may. The actual submission deadline is in late June; however, you should submit as early as possible to avoid last-minute panicking and to maximise exposure to your work. You may wish to ask a teacher to mentor you, as they look for pieces that are very comprehensive and polished. Competition is fierce! Plan 3 months in advance.  Be sure to also check their formatting requirements!

Title: ABLA
Description: ABLA, also known as the Associação de Beneficência Luso-Alemã, was founded in the 1980s by a group of Germans moved by the plight of refugees from former Portuguese colonies. As a non-profit and development NGO, ABLA supports people of all ages locally and internationally, aiming to foster individual potential and build a fairer society. Its work focuses on combating social exclusion, domestic violence, school dropout, and elderly isolation, supported by donations, partnerships, and community initiatives. You can contribute to their mission by being a volunteer in the free daycare centre, where small children from economically underprivileged families are being looked after, or you could provide a helping hand in supporting senior citizens (aged 65+).
HowToApply: Contact the association via email to ask if they have any volunteer positions open. You should do so around 3 months in advance, to ensure that you'd have a spot there.

Title: Cercica
HowToApply: Contact the association via email to ask if they have any volunteer positions open. You should do so around 3 months in advance, to ensure that you'd have a spot there.

Title: Treetree2.school
AgeGroup: CLS 6, CLS 7, CLS 8, IG1, IG2, IB1, IB2
Description: See what's beyond school with treetree2's program ---- afterschool. They have an array of programs catered towards people in middle school and highschool, where you can explore an area of your interest by spending some of your free time going through the materials sent to you in the program, and a few hours of accompanied studying. Their wide range of programs allows you to dive into different areas, from genetics to artificial intelligence and calculus. If you're unsure about the area you want to pursue, this is the program for you.
HowToApply: Submit your application by registering an account on their website, then enrol in the course that you're interested in. Applications are open every month. If you have any doubts, contact them via email afterschool@treetree2.org.

Title: General internships
Category: Computer science, Biology, Biomed, Veterinary sciences, Art, Volunteering, Finance, Mathematics, Chemistry, Physics, Robotics, Humanities
Description: You can get experience with any area of interest by contacting companies/institutions for an internship. Be specific about what you want to do there, and how long you plan to stay (ie. an observational internship for 2 weeks) For instance: banks, law firms, research institutions, humanitarian associations...
HowToApply: In portugal, there are many insitutions/companies of interest that you can contact. Those that have educational purposes are more likely admit you.  | International Iberian Nanotechnology Laboratory | Jose de Mello Saude | Rede de Quimica e Tecnologia | Instituto Portugues do Mar e da Atmosfera | Centro de Investigacao em Biodiversidade e Recursos Geneticos | Laboratory of Instrumentation and Experimental Particle Physics | National Laboratory for Civil Engineering | Portuguese Environment Agency (APA) | UN Portugal & affiliated institutes |

Title: Astro Ovo
Description: - Organized in Portugal to promote astronomy and space to younger students (lower secondary). - Activities usually include astronomy workshops, competitions, sky observations, and hands-on projects. - Focus: spark curiosity about physics and space.
HowToApply: Contact your science/physics teacher to check if your school is participating. Applications are made by schools via Portugal Space or local educational networks. Students cannot apply individually.

Title: Astro Pi
Description: - Run by the European Space Agency (ESA) and the Raspberry Pi Foundation. - Students can code experiments that run on real Raspberry Pi computers aboard the ISS. - Two tracks: • Mission Zero (simple coding, no selection process). • Mission Space Lab (advanced coding + project).
HowToApply: Talk to your ICT/computer science teacher or club leader. Teachers register student teams on the Astro Pi Challenge website. You cannot apply individually; you need a supervising teacher/mentor.

Title: CANSAT Junior
Description: - Educational program in Portugal introducing students to space engineering concepts. - Students build a 'CanSat' using a soda can to simulate the functions of a real satellite (sensors, parachutes, data collection). - Designed to inspire interest in STEM early on.
HowToApply: Speak with your science/technology teacher. Schools register teams through the Portuguese Space Agency (Portugal Space). Teachers handle the application process.

Title: CANSAT Portugal
Description: - National competition for secondary school students. - Teams design, build, and test a mini-satellite that must fit inside a soda can and perform a mission (e.g., measure temperature, pressure, GPS tracking). - Culminates in a rocket launch and mission presentation.
HowToApply: Ask your physics or engineering teacher if your school participates. Teams must apply through the Portugal Space CanSat portal. Applications are only accepted via a teacher supervisor.

Title: Climate detectives
Description: - ESA initiative where students research a real environmental/climate issue using satellite data, weather data, or measurements they collect. - Goals: understand Earth’s climate, scientific inquiry, teamwork. - Students share results internationally on ESA’s platform.
HowToApply: Tell your science/geography teacher about the program. Teachers create and register teams on the ESA Climate Detectives platform. Students cannot register individually.

Title: Mission X
Description: - ESA program: “Train Like an Astronaut”. - Combines physical activities (fitness, nutrition, teamwork) with STEM challenges. - Focus on healthy lifestyle, teamwork, and astronaut training methods. - Targeted at younger students (lower secondary).
HowToApply: Talk to your PE or science teacher. Schools/teachers must sign up classes on the Mission X website. Students cannot sign up directly.

Title: Moon Camp challenge
Description: - Organized by ESA & Airbus Foundation. - Students design a moon base considering life-support, shelter, energy, and sustainability. - Levels: • Discovery (7–9, entry-level). • Explorers (intermediate, design in 3D). • Pioneers (advanced).
HowToApply: Ask your science/technology teacher to form a team. Teachers register teams on the Moon Camp Challenge website. You’ll need a teacher supervisor to submit your project and 3D models.

Title: Breakthrough Junior challenge
Category: Physics, Computer science, Mathematics, Biology, Chemistry
Description: The breakthrough junior challenge is an international competition based in the US, but submissions are made every year by students all over the world. The challenge involves the creation of a short video on a science/math topic of your choice, explaining the concept creatively. Winners are eligible for monetary prizes and scholarships.
HowToApply: Register an account on the website, registrations open around May. Submission of videos have a endline at the beginning of september.

Title: Hubro Championship
Description: Hubro simulations provide educational resources on finance and marketing. This company hosts a contest called the hubro championship, in which students across the globe run virtual simulations of businesses. Participants run their own virtual manufacturing companies, using in-game data to make business decisions, including tasks within market forecasting, R&D, production, contracting, financing and budgeting. Participants compete with companies run by their peers, in the same virtual market.
HowToApply: The company which owns hubro championships is called hubro simulations. They work in partnerships with schools and educators. You'll need to ask your business/economics teacher to join as a member, in order for you and your peers to participate in these simulations. Ideally, these would be paid by the school. Alternatively, you could submit a proposal to your teachers, for the school to adopt these simulations. Otherwise, you could start your own business club at school with a supervisor, and the club would fund the simulations.

Title: MILL
Description: MILL stands for Makers In Little Lisbon. MILL is home to an ever-changing, diverse collective of artists, designers and technologists, and hub for a wider community of collaborators, working together to bring all kinds of projects to life. They regularly host workshops on digital art, technology, animation, and so on. MILL has the technology and the expert artists to make digital art come to life. Whether you are looking for assistance on a project, or just want to help out and learn some new skills, MILL accommodates all sorts of collaborations.
HowToApply: If you are an art student who's interested in MILL, you should send an email to the studio. Some possible ideas for engaging with MILL: attending workshops hosted by MILL--this would be a great way to connect with the artists/experts there; ask to be an assistant intern to ongoing projects at MILL; if you have an idea in mind that involves combining technology and art, such as that involving moving mechanical parts or lights that shine in a particular rythm, you can seek the help of experts at MILL. Just email them!

Title: Boy/Girl Scouts
Description: Learn by getting hands on with group based project work! Scouting involves activities such as constructions, games, exploration, raids or community service, depending on which age group you're in. Scouting is meant to reflect the challenges that young people encounter throughout their life. This could be a great way to make friends outside of school.
HowToApply: There's a map on their website displaying local groups and their contact information. Send an email to request more information, or to indicate your wish to join them. Each scout will need to pay for insurance and administrative fees, with the amount depending on the local group's budget.

Title: WSDC
Description: The World Schools Debating Championships (WSDC) is a global competition for high school debaters. It is held annually and involves more than 70 national teams. Teams from all over the world discuss a variety of different topics from international relations to economics and sport. It's an in-person event, with each country sending a team of max. 5 people to represent them.
HowToApply: Contact the local Portuguese branch to find out how you could be selected to represent portugal. | National Organisation: International Portuguese Debate Association. | Contact Name: Gabriel Guia | Contact Email Address:associacaoidportugal@gmail.com

Title: European youth debate events
Description: Your Europe, Your Say! (YEYS) is the annual youth event of the EESC, aiming to connect young people from all EU Member States and from the candidate countries with the European Union. Selected participants go to Brussels to take part in the event. They discuss and work together to come up with resolutions which reflect their ideas as future European citizens; the EESC then shares these resolutions with the EU institutions and highlights them at other key events.
HowToApply: Every year, the YEYS hosts an open call for participating schools with debate teams of students aged 16-18. Applications end by November, and conferences take place around march. You'll need a supervising teacher to submit an application that represents the whole school.

Title: University of Lisbon summer school
Category: Computer science, Biology, Biomed, Veterinary sciences, Art, Volunteering, Finance, Mathematics, Chemistry, Physics, Robotics, Humanities
Description: 1st week -- [Grade 11 & 12] ; 2nd week--Grade 8, 9, 10. Summer at ULisboa provides young people with the opportunity to experience academic life.

For a week, each School of the University of Lisbon (ULisboa) offers a complete plan of activities (experiments, games, visits and workshops) to show the basic knowledge, practical tasks and working methods of the courses taught at ULisboa. Various faculties are involved in the program, see detailed information on their website for the faculty you're interested in. Eg. Art, Sciences, Social Sciences, Law, etc.
HowToApply: Applications open on the Verão na ULisboa website, around the beginning of May. You'll need a motivation letter, a CV, and other materials such as transcripts as per the requirements of each faculty. There is a participation fee of 250 euros. See the third link for all the faculties in ULisboa.

Title: Nova Lisbon University summer school
Description: Some insane summer school that costs 3000-3500 euros. Covers accommodation and 3 meals a day.
HowToApply: Application form is on their website.

Title: Marine biology -- Educational programs
Description: MARE promotes the interaction of its researchers with the non-academic community through dedicated science communication and environmental education programs.
MARE goes to school: Scientists of Portuguese universities educate students on marine biology (hands-on activities, interactive games, lab activities, mystery boxes, field trips).
Kids Dive: Dynamic program including guided aquarium tours, workshops, field trips with researchers, biodiversity workshops, and summit Kids Dive.
Semanas Polares (Polar Weeks): Workshops on the studies of polar regions hosted by scientists and professors.
SERMARE: Team of researchers from the University of Coimbra dedicated to promoting ocean literacy through collaborations with schools and teachers.
bLueTIDE: Initiative focused on biodiversity conservation in rocky intertidal areas.
MARE STARTUP: Collaboration between Universidade de Lisboa and Católica focused on innovative entrepreneurship and start-up incubators based on marine science and technology.
MarSemLixo: Volunteer project collecting waste from fishing activities and providing infrastructure to process it, involving fishermen and public and private entities.
HowToApply: Explore MARE's diverse educational programs. You could monitor them regularly to see when annual events are occuring. Better yet, you can contact the organisation/intiative directly (see each website for emails/phone numbers), to host a program at your school. Eg. Contact your biology teacher to coordinate a biodiversity-themed field trip to rocky tidal areas, guided by MARE researchers. Find the links for selected active programs in the linked page from MARE.

Title: CE3C
Description: Centre for ecology, evolution and environmental changes. CE3C is composed of 39 Research Groups, nested into four Research Areas. Ecology and Conservation Across Scales; Evolutionary Perspectives in a Changing World; Environment and Health Interactions; and Towards Sustainable and Resilient Ecosystems. Different techniques are used in their research, affiliated with computer science (see computational ecology) and chemistry. Ecosystems are intrinsically linked to human societies, CE3C has research groups dedicated to interdisciplinary research where science intersects with humanities/social science. Explore their webpage for more information on current events being hosted (check out the upcoming SER2027 Lisboa). They seem very welcoming towards initiatives on public outreach and education.
HowToApply: There are many possible ways in which you can get involved with CE3C, but all of them start with sending that first email. Read through webpages that introduce the research groups and affiliated facilities, get to know them well first, and have a clear idea of what you would like to collaborate on.
Ask if you could intern for a short period at their research laboratory.
Invite a researcher whose work intrigues you to speak at your school.
Devise a science fair themed on the focus of CE3C’s research (ecology, environmental science, biodiversity, conservation, evolution, etc.).
Ask if there are opportunities to volunteer at upcoming events (e.g., conferences or data collection projects with public participation).
Ask if there are educational programs the organisation hosted in the past that could be recreated on a smaller scale (e.g., locally at your school or in your class).

Organise a bake sale to raise funds for an initiative such as restoring native plant species to the school garden, hosting a biodiversity infographic contest, or any creative project that raises awareness within your community.

Title: University of Porto Physics Academy
Description: The Physics Academy is an activity aimed at Physics students of the 11th and 12th grade and provides a unique and challenging experience in the academic world of the University of Porto.

The main objective is to introduce students to what it means to do science in Physics, Physical Engineering and Astronomy. The program has a strong computational and laboratory component, complementing current secondary education programs. It is intended that the training acquired is useful in students' academic careers. The program is free of charge and takes place at the Faculty of Sciences in the Physics and Astronomy Department every Friday from late February to early April.
HowToApply: You'll need to ask your teacher for a letter of recommendation according to a specific model on their website. Then, fill out the application form on their website.

Title: University of Porto Junior University
Category: Computer science, Biology, Biomed, Veterinary sciences, Art, Volunteering, Finance, Mathematics, Chemistry, Physics, Robotics, Humanities, Languages
Description: Summer programs at the University of Porto, lasting anywhere from 1 day to 2 weeks.
- Experiment in the summer: 5th and 6th grade. Four programmes spanning a wide range of areas: Art, biology, dentistry, chemistry, history, mathematics, sports, philosophy, landscape architecture.

- Summer Workshops: 7th and 8th grades. Five programs including areas: Art, biology, sports, philosophy, mathematics, economics, art history, chemistry, astronomy, geology, history, psychology, archeology
- Thematic Workshops: 7th and 8th grades. Themes include: Architecture, civil engineering / urban planning, life and heath, art, sports — athletics.
- Summer projects: 9th, 10th, 11th. There’s one for every department imaginable. You will definitely find a project that interests you.
- Language School: 5th to 11th. Learn through games, debates, and other fun activities. German, Spanish, Japanese, Mandarin, English, Latin and Greek, French.
- Introduction to Research. 3 programs: Chemistry and biochemistry, 10th to 12th; Life sciences and health sciences, 10th and 11th; Physics, 10th to 12th.

- Seasonal program: Engineer league, special junior university. Joint collab between Northern Engineers’ association and U of Porto. 3D print a radio-controlled car prototype, enter a competition. Aimed at high school students.
HowToApply: Complete registration on their website, then sign up using the link on the page of your selected program. You'll need a letter of motivation, and a digitalised copy of your most recent grades.

Title: University of Porto Sciences department summer school
Description: Summer School of Chemistry and Biochemistry: You'll have the opportunity to join a research team and take part in a relevant and current project. You will collaborate with some of the most outstanding researchers in the field and familiarise yourself with the latest discoveries and innovations in Chemistry and Biochemistry. Aimed at students from 10th to 12th grade.

Summer School of Physics: Experience what it’s like to be a physicist or physics engineer. Includes advanced courses in Relativity, Astrophysics, Quantum Mechanics and Nanotechnologies that stimulate reflection on your perception of reality; team projects on concrete problems whose solution will be presented at the end of the program. Targeted to high school students, with priority given to 11th grade.

Summer School of Mathematics: Participants will have a first opportunity to learn mathematics topics not included in current high school programs. Includes problem-solving and computational experimentation. The topics are taught in mini-courses. Useful for those wishing to pursue exact sciences (maths, physics), engineering, economics, natural sciences (biology), etc. Aimed at students from 10th to 12th grade.
HowToApply: Complete registration on their website, then sign up using the link specific to each program. You'll need a letter of motivation.

Title: Porto Institute of Philosophy Summer School
Description: The school’s program will feature lectures by invited speakers, student presentations, group discussions, one-on-one tutorials, and social program. Invitations for submissions of proposals for 30-minute presentations followed by 15-minute discussion. Along with the title, the abstract (up to 600 words), references and 5 keywords, the proposal should include a short biographical note.
HowToApply: The event is aimed for graduate students. However, you could try to ask if it would be possible for you to just be there and observe the event, without submitting an actual application. Email address in on the linked site.

Title: AMC contest Europe
Description: AMC stands for American Mathematics Contest. The contest consists of 25 multiple choice questions completed under timed conditions, using mathematical problem solving skills that go beyond the standard school syllabus. College admissions offices love to see problem-solving skills on applications. Students with top scores on the AMC 10 or 12 may be invited to take the American Invitational Mathematics Exam (AIME).
HowToApply: The AMC has 3 categories: AMC8, students in 8th grade or below, max. age 15.5 years; AMC 10, students in 10th grade or below, max. age 17.5; AMC12, students in 12th grade or below, max. age 19.5. AMC contests are hosted by schools, contact your mathematics teacher and class director to register the school for AMC Europe. Details for registration can be found in linked site.

Title: Royal Society of Biology Photography Competition
Description:
The Young Photographer of the Year category is open to RSB members and non-members aged 18 years or under.
Entrants for both categories can live in the UK or globally.
The competition entries will be shortlisted by RSB staff, and then opened to a public vote.
Shortlisted entrants will be notified in early summer. Every year, the Royal Society of Biology sets a different competition theme for contestants to showcase their work. the Young Photographer of the Year winner's prize will be £500.
The winners will also be invited to attend the Biology Week Awards Ceremony in London in October.
HowToApply: Sign up to the contest through the official website linked below, and enter up to 6 photosgraphs.

Title: The Young Coders competition
Description: The Young Coders Competition is open for children aged 8 to 13 years old - who can work individually or in teams of 2 - 6 children to create their project.The contest is based in the UK, but international entries are allowed. The competition is to create a project using Scratch.
HowToApply: Competing teams need an adult supervisor to register them on the linked site and receive the necessary materials. Contact your computer science teacher for further support and guidance.

Title: BioArtAttack 2D (Nancy Rothwell Prize)
Description: Nancy Rothwell Prize\nThis prize is open to all young people aged 7-18 years old from anywhere in the world. The Nancy Rothwell Prize is a sub-category specifically for specimen drawings entered into this competition — these can be of any animal, plant, or fungi. Competition entries into this category need to include labels and descriptions on the artwork itself.
HowToApply: See terms and conditions on linked site. Entrants under 13 will need parent/guardian consent. Submit your work through the linked site when the submission date opens.

Title: BioCraft Competition
Description: In this competition, participants create a biology-inspired 3D artwork from recycled, or other, materials. The competition is open to anyone in the UK and internationally. Entries are free of charge and the competition is open to all ages.
HowToApply: See terms and conditions on linked site. Entrants under 13 will need parent/guardian consent. Submit your work through the linked site when the submission date opens.

Title: Cambridge Upper Secondary Science Competition
Description: The Science Competition is for teams of three to six students studying Cambridge IGCSE and Cambridge O Level science. Teams choose a topic and work on a scientific investigation over 20-25 hours. At the end of the project, students will present their findings, giving them the opportunity to communicate their work to a wider audience. This could be in the form of a science fair at school, team presentations or posters. Teams will also reflect on the way they have worked together.
HowToApply: The school would have to register for competition entry. Contact your IG teachers in the science department. The competition is currently paused for 2026, but future editions may occur.

Title: Teen Tech Awards
Description: The TeenTech Awards are for UK students from 11-16 (Years 7 to 11) and 17-19 (Year 12-13) working in teams of up to three to look at problems large and small to see if they can find a better way of doing things. Entries may be made by international teams by prior arrangement with TeenTech Charity. Please email kay.sawbridge@teentech.com. And you don’t have to work on your own! You can contact companies, universities, colleges and even join up with a group of students on the other side of the world to work with you on your idea. TeenTech will also connect you with an industry mentor to help support you with your project.
HowToApply: Ask your school to communicate with the Teentech charity in advance, to see if there are any possibilities of making an entry or collaborating with a uk team. Further details can be found in linked site.

Title: Imperial college Science and Innovation Competition
Description:
The International Science & Innovation Competition, which is run by the Faculty of Natural Sciences at Imperial College, is open to teams of young people of secondary school age based internationally outside of the UK. Teams will be asked to develop a new and innovative scientific solution to help achieve one of the United Nation’s Global Goals for Sustainable Development. To enter, teams will be asked to create a 5-minute film that:
- Briefly outlines the goal they have selected
- Describes their innovative solution
- Explains the science behind their idea
- Illustrates how their idea would address the goal.
HowToApply: Teams must consist of 2 - 4 individuals, with a teacher, group leader or other responsible adult acting as a mentor and point of contact. The competition opens in September and teams are pre-registered by January and would then submit their formal entry to the competition by February.

Title: NSDA debate contest
Description: Speech and debate is a co-curricular activity that empowers young people to research, discuss, and deliberate over important and timely issues. At every tournament, students present their ideas before judges—ranging from parents and community leaders to alumni and educators—and strive to persuade through logic, evidence, and style.
HowToApply: Your school needs to be a member of this organisation's International Program. Talk to your humanities subject teachers about making a proposal to the head of your middle/high school. See further details on linked site.

Title: Online research program
Description: Non-Trivial is a global, online program where high school students (age 14-20) learn research skills, explore impactful topics (e.g. climate, AI, policy), and build independent projects through guided coursework, mentorship, and peer collaboration. The program is free, selective, and can include grants or scholarships for outstanding work.
HowToApply: Register yourself on their website and fill out your application form. Keep an eye out for application deadlines.

Title: RSI (Research Science Institute) summer program at MIT
Description: Each summer, 100 of the world's most accomplished high school students gather at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) for the Research Science Institute (RSI). RSI is the first cost-free to students, summer science & engineering program to combine on-campus course work in scientific theory with off-campus work in science and technology research. RSI scholars first participate in a week of intensive STEM classes with accomplished professors. The heart of RSI is the five week research internship where students conduct individual projects under the tutelage of mentors who are experienced scientists and researchers.
HowToApply: Typically, students apply in the beginning of their IB1 academic year. See linked site for application details.

Title: MIT Reality Hack 2026 · Barcelona Node
Description:
MIT Reality Hack is conceived as an open and inclusive hackathon that challenges conventional formats. Rather than focusing solely on technical outcomes, it emphasizes learning, collaboration, and experimentation across disciplines and levels of experience. The event is designed for first-time participants, seasoned hackers, and those who wish to engage in alternative or exploratory ways.

Projects developed during the hackathon may rethink the hackathon format itself, address social and cultural challenges, or result in interactive, performative, or experiential works. Traditionally, the hackathon unfolds across three main thematic directions:

Hack the Hack – designing new, more inclusive and accessible hackathon models 
Hack the Community – creating technologies that strengthen communities or foster positive social impact 
Hack the Party – developing playful, interactive installations and shared experiences 
Alongside these, participants are also free to pursue any idea without thematic constraints.
HowToApply: Submit an application through their registration site.

Title: Atlantic Junior (Science fair style competition)
Description:
The ATLÂNTICO JÚNIOR competition promotes scientific and technological culture by highlighting the Atlantic Ocean’s role in planetary sustainability and society. Inspired by the FLAD Science Award Atlantic, it encourages younger students to engage in hands-on, team-based learning focused on technology and ocean-related challenges.
For the 2025/2026 edition, projects are invited in areas such as:
Marine renewable energy (e.g., wave/tidal devices, sensor-equipped marine robots)
Innovative vessels (e.g., solar-powered remote-controlled boats)
Technologies to reduce marine pollution
Solutions for marine biodiversity conservation and ecosystem regeneration
Finalist teams will present their work on June 6, 2026, including a public presentation, prototype demonstration, and poster.
HowToApply: Projects are submitted by each group (max. 5 people), supervised by a teacher. Teams should seek partnerships with universities, polytechnic institutes, and/or companies for technical and scientific support. Registration and submission in linked site. Be sure to read the pdf of regulations.

Title: Solar Sailors Challenge
Description: This is a solar-powered boat competition designed to raise awareness among high school students about the importance of green energy transition. During the competition, students will be invited to test a small prototype boat they have built using recycled materials, powered exclusively by electricity generated by solar cells.
HowToApply: Sign up to the contest through the official website linked below.

Title: Portuguese competence centre in semiconductors (POEMS) — Semiconductors at school
Description: POEMS is the Portuguese Competence Centre in Semiconductors, a cutting-edge action under the Chips for Europe Initiative. The Semiconductors at School programme brings microelectronics and semiconductor science into basic and secondary classrooms through hands-on activities, interactive demonstrations, and teacher training. Its goal is to spark curiosity among young people and bridge the future talent gap in this fast-growing sector. POEMS is developing educational kits that allow students to explore the principles of microelectronics and semiconductors through practical experiments, creative challenges and discussion opportunities.
HowToApply:
There are many possible ways in which you can get involved with POEMS, but all of them start with sending that first email! Read through webpages that introduce the research groups and affiliated facilities, get to know them well first, and have a clear idea of what you would like to collaborate on. Get creative with anything that raises awareness within your community, reach out to the researchers/institutions/experts whose work interests you.

- Ask if you could intern shortly at one of their facilities
- Invite an expert whose work intrigues you to speak at your school
- Devise a science fair themed on the focus of POEMS's work (semiconductors, technology & entrepreneurship, chip design & development, etc.)
- Ask if there are opportunities to volunteer at upcoming events (ie. conferences, educational initiatives)
- Ask if there are educational programs that the organisation has hosted in the past that could be brought back in a smaller scale (ie. local to your school or your class)
- Bake sale or any other fundraiser for the event you have planned in mind.

Title: Food 2030
Description: Funded by the European Union, the CLEVERFOOD project engages all sectors of society in transforming Europe’s food system. Its goal is to build a fair, healthy, and sustainable food system by supporting projects, partnerships, and networks through a collaborative approach. You can make your own contribution to these ongoing initiatives by raising awareness on the importance of sustainable food systems.
HowToApply: Food2030 has made a variety of toolkits available, aimed at increasing outreach and public engagement. You can use them to help develop your own event or initiative, then host them at school [see link 2]. Since one of the primary goals of Food2030 is to educate the public, don't shy away from emailing them for help with anything you need.

Title: Blue lights schools
Description: BlueLightS is a Horizon Europe project strengthening the knowledge and skills of European children and youth to deliver ‘blue’ (river and seas) resilience and sustainability. The project collaborates closely with projects such as ProBleu, SHORE, PREP4BLUE and the UN Ocean Decade. The BlueLightS website features a wide range of resources in their knowledge hub, from educational resources to articles targeted at concerned citizens. You can use those resources to educate yourself and your community.
HowToApply: You can refer to the cienciaviva website for inspiration on past BlueLightS events, and come up with a creative proposal for your school. Introduce this idea to your science teachers to see if the school can accommodate your idea, or even join the official initiative as another BlueLightS school. The initiative encourages bottom-up approaches, so don't be afraid to email them or other relevant organisations for help!

Title: Junior Achievement Portugal
Description: Founded in 2005, Junior Achievement Portugal (JAP) is a non-profit organization and the Portuguese counterpart of JA Worldwide, an organization nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize and which ranks 5thº among the Social Good Organizations with the greatest impact in the world. They lead initiatives to educate students of all ages on entrepreneurship, volunteerism, and innovative businesses.
HowToApply: For secondary school students, JAP has 3 different initiatives. ''The Company'' is the flagship program of Junior Achievement through which high school students create a mini-enterprise with the help of a volunteer and a teacher. Students brainstorm in search of an innovative idea to respond to an identified problem or need, develop the product or service and present a business plan for their mini-enterprise. ''The Innovation Challenge'' consists of solving a challenge launched and that is worked on in just 8 hours. This challenge focuses on innovative and creative processes based on real business challenges that companies have to deal with in their day-to-day life. ''The Right Arm'' is a day in which High School students accompany volunteers in their work environment and participate in their daily activities. Through this practical experience, students acquire knowledge about culture, work ethics and the various existing career options. Ask a teacher for supervision and sign up using the registration portal on the linked site.

Title: Portugal Students Prize
Description: The sovereign art foundation hosts an annual art competition for students in secondary schooling. Check out other initiatives and programs from the foundation using the linked site.
HowToApply: The Portugal Students Prize is open to all students enrolled in secondary school in Portugal, aged 11-18 years old (at the time of entry). Participating students must be nominated by their teacher. Teachers can nominate up to 10 students to enter. Upon entry, students will be asked to enter the name of their nominating teacher. Nominated students can submit one artwork each online before the submission deadline. The Judges Prize of €500 is awarded to the student artist with the highest score from the judges. €1,500 is awarded to the school of the Judges Prize winner.\nThe Public Vote Prize of €300 is awarded to the student artist with the most votes from the public. €800 is awarded to the school of the Public Vote Prize Winner.

Title: Lisbon Project
Description: The Lisbon Project is a non-profit organisation dedicated to integrate and empower immigrants and refugees.
HowToApply: Volunteer vacancies are listed on the linked website. Check your eligibility and submit and application. You will most likely need to volunteer in person at least once a week.

Title: SEED: creating young entrepreneurs
Description: Lisbon City Council prioritises entrepreneurship and innovation. This led to the SEED project, which challenges students, teachers, and entrepreneurial ecosystem partners to promote the development of entrepreneurial skills among young people (9th to 12th grade), to create opportunities for them to engage with Lisbon's innovation and creativity.
HowToApply:
Propose the idea of involving your school in this project to your teachers. SEED brings the following to schools:                                 Creating an entrepreneurship lab.
Organising study visits to entrepreneurial spaces and promoting workshops.
Conducting financial literacy sessions.
Supporting the development of a project created by the students.
Encouraging the development of skills and career path choices.
Facilitating training experiences within the entrepreneurial ecosystem.

Title: Fontenova theatre studio
Description: Teatro Estúdio Fontenova is based in setubal. They offer theater workshops.
HowToApply: See linked site for contact information, email or call to ask if there are vacancies available in the workshops.

Title: Aequalitas
Description: The Association for Equality AEQUALITAS is an NGO committed to creating a more just and sustainable world. Their main goal is to achieve social equity, promoting the specific rights and interests of minorities and the most disadvantaged groups through the creation and implementation of projects and initiatives that contribute to the empowerment of target audiences.
HowToApply: You can support this organisation by organising an event, volunteer work, or raise funds. See linked site for details.

Title: Youth volunteers for nature and forests
Description: Promote youth volunteering practices in the field of nature preservation, forests and their ecosystems, through awareness of populations in general, as well as prevention against forest fires and other disasters with environmental impact, monitoring and recovery of affected territories.
HowToApply: Sign up and register yourself on linked site to volunteer. There may be a selection process.

Title: Now us
Description: Personalised volunteering activities. It is a Volunteer Program aimed at young people who wish, through the practice of volunteering, to actively intervene in their community while acquiring skills.
HowToApply: Sign up and register yourself on linked site. There may be a selection process.

Title: Dating with fair play
HowToApply: Sign up and register yourself on linked site. There may be a selection process.

Title: Holidays with movement
Description:
The Moving Vacation Program was created so that you can carry out activities and have fun with other young people your age, during the Easter or summer holidays. The activities take place in Summer Camps and are very varied.

There are two modes of operation: one, which has the same name as the program,

which has thematic areas such as, for example, among others, the Environment, Culture or Sport;

the other modality, called Vacation 5.0, integrates Robotics and Programming,

Artificial Intelligence and Digital Communication.

There are residential and non-residential holiday camps. Participation is totally free.
HowToApply: Sign up and register yourself on linked site. There may be a selection process.

Title: Floobynooby
Description: Free courses on character design, animation, storyboard
HowToApply: Open sourced, see linked site.

Title: Temple of the seven golden camels
Description: Learning resources for storyboarding.
HowToApply: Open sourced, see linked site.

Title: Energymag Virtual Internships
Description: EnergyMag offers a limited number of virtual internships to both college and high school students who want to make the world a better place by increasing the share of renewable energy in the world, learn about the energy storage industry, and get work experience in an exciting and fast-moving environment.
HowToApply: See details, eligibility requirements, and application form on linked site. Don't worry too much about the subject/grade requirements, you can explain that your school doesn't follow the US education system,.

Title: Mathquantum fellowships
Description:
Mentored research and interdisciplinary training in applying mathematical methods to QIS problems.
 Coursework and seminars in theoretical and applied mathematics for QIS.
 Internship opportunities at quantum companies and government labs.
 Professional development and networking opportunities (including on Broader Impacts and proposal writing).
 One-on-one career mentoring and guidance.
 Community-building and outreach activities.
HowToApply: High school fellowships are offered during summer to about 10 students, who will participate together as a cohort. Fellowships consist of a ~2-week online program of math and quantum information science lectures, seminars, professional development workshops, hands-on activities, discussions, and a mini-project. There are spring, summer, and fall sessions. See linked site for application components (transcripts, reference letters, etc).

Title: Foreign policy research institute (FRPI) Research/Administrations internships
Description: Research Interns work under the direct supervision of an FPRI staffer and alongside scholars in one of the following research areas: Eurasia, Asia, and National Security. Duties and availability vary between programs, so applicants should specify which research program they are interested in and will be matched with a scholar in that program upon acceptance. This position currently allows for remote interns who can communicate with their supervisor via Zoom, email, and/or phone. Research internships often require advanced foreign language skills, data analysis skills, and study abroad or field research experience. Administration & Operations Interns will assist FPRI’s management staff with a variety of projects related to our internship program, and other operations-related tasks.
HowToApply: See linked site for application details. You'll increase your chances of being selected if you apply for an unpaid, part-time internship.

Title: Lumiere Foundation’s Humanities and Social Sciences Program
Description: In the Lumiere Humanities and Social Sciences Program, students can do research in fields like sociology, history, languages, philosophy, education or public policy. 12 week research program, 9 individual sessions with world-class researcher, 2 individual sessions with writing coach, 5 workshops on research process, 15-page research paper.
HowToApply: Fill out an application form from linked site.

Title: Lumiere Breakthrough Scholar Program
Description: The Lumiere Breakthrough Scholar Program pairs talented high-school students with world-class PhD mentors to work 1-on-1 on an independent research project. At the end of the 12-week program, you’ll learn about the cutting edge of your field and develop an independent research paper. 12 week research program, 9 individual sessions with world-class researcher, 2 individual sessions with writing coach, 5 workshops on research process, 15-page research paper.
HowToApply: Fill out an application form from linked site.

Title: Rockerfeller university -- Summer Science Research Program
Description: SSRP Scholars will participate on a research team designed and guided by scientific trainees from the Tri-Institutions (Rockefeller, MSK, and WCMC). SSRP teams mirror the structure of a research laboratory, where there are 1-3 lead team leads who are supported by additional scientists. Each team will have their own space and resources in one of the dedicated RockEDU teaching laboratories. The SSRP experience is enhanced by relevant elective courses, guest lectures, workshops, networking opportunities, and social events.
HowToApply: See application directions on linked site.

Title: Cambridge Re:think Essay Competition
Description: The spirit of the Cambridge Re:think essay competition is to encourage critical thinking and exploration of a wide range of thought-provoking and often controversial topics. The competition covers a diverse array of subjects, from historical and present issues to speculative future scenarios. Participants are invited to engage deeply with these topics, critically analysing their various facets and implications. 2000 words excluding citations & footnotes.
HowToApply: Each year, different topics are assigned. See linked site for eligibility, guidelines, and submission portal. The competition opens in January and submission deadline is in May,

Title: Bennington College Young Writers Awards
Description: High school students worldwide are invited to submit poetry, fiction, or nonfiction entries for a chance to win cash, a scholarship, and prestige.
HowToApply: See linked site for the most up to date regulations, and the submission portal.

Title: Horizon Academic Essay Prize
Description: The objective of the Horizon Academic Essay Prize is to push young students to critically engage with the world around them. Participants are expected to dive deep into their chosen questions - showcase independent thought, clear argumentation, and rigorous analysis. 1000 word essay.
HowToApply: Topics are renewed for each year's contest. You'll need a teacher to be your referee. See linked site for application and submission form. Deadline for submission: beginning of February.

Title: Jane Austen Society of North America -- Essay Contest
Description: JASNA conducts an annual student Essay Contest to encourage the study and appreciation of Jane Austen's works in new generations of readers. 6-8 pages long, focused on personal interpretation and literary analysis. Suggested topics vary by year.
HowToApply: See linked site for detailed guidelines, registration and regulations. Submissions open in February and end in June.

Title: Jane Austen Society of North America -- Young Filmmakers Contest
Description: Enter an original short film of 5 minutes or less in JASNA's annual Young Filmmakers Contest—a creative competition designed to encourage the study, interpretation, and appreciation of Jane Austen among new generations. Prescribed themes vary by year.
HowToApply: See linked site for detailed guidelines, registration and regulations. Submissions end in June.

Title: Northeastern University London -- AI Futures Challenge
Description: A five-day online innovation challenge for Year 12 students, enabling students to gain experience of working on a real-life business challenge set by one of their start-up hub businesses. Participants work individually or in teams of 2-3 to address a business-sponsored challenge using AI, receiving academic support and feedback. Solve an industry-led business challenge with AI, supported by specialists. Develop practical skills, networks and digital credentials to gain a competitive edge in university applications. Learn how different disciplines work together to create better solutions.
HowToApply: Students in IB1 can enter the challenge as a team or individually. Registration and guidelines in linked site.

Title: High School Academic Research Competition
Description: Running through April and May, SARC is more than just a contest—it’s a guided immersion into the research process, designed for experts and newcomers alike! You’ll enroll in immersive workshops that guide you through the research process from beginning to end and prepare you for your own research proposal. SARC is unique in that participants can research any topic they want! Researchers can choose a topic they’re passionate about, something they wish to dive deeper into, or something they want to study in university.
HowToApply: SARC is open to all high school students aged 13-18. See linked site for registration and details. To participate, all you need to do is fill in a quick registration form and follow the required guidelines.

Title: Tumo Portugal
Description: The TUMO learning program is completely free and consists of self-learning activities, workshops and advanced laboratories in eight learning areas: programming, animation, graphic design, video game development, cinema, robotics, music, photography.
HowToApply: Have your parent/guardian fill out a simple registration form from the linked site, then attend a scheduled tour of the tumo center.

Title: Náboj international physics competition
Description: Náboj Physics is a team competition for high-school students and students of corresponding grades of 8-year grammar schools. Teams consist of four or five members. All members of any single team come from the same school. The contestants can solve any of the eight problems available. The result is usually a single numeric value or a mathematical expression. The competition lasts two hours. Junior category is open to those below the IB level. Senior category is open to all.
HowToApply: Teams sign up for the competition via the web site during the registration period. Any single school can register at most two teams into each category. Any other team from the same school is placed on the waiting list, and it will be involved in the competition only if the venue’s full capacity is not reached at the end of the registration period. Ask your physics teacher to coordinate with the school for registration.

Title: BSMS Virtual Work Experience
Description: This course provides an online work experience for those considering to apply to medical school. You will be taken through six areas of medicine, exploring the roles, skills required, and challenges faced by today's doctors.
HowToApply: Create an account on the linked site.

Title: MIT Portugal
Description: The MIT Portugal Program (MPP) is a strategic international partnership between Portuguese universities and research institutions, the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), the Portuguese government, as well as partners from industry and other non-academic institutions.
HowToApply: Keep an eye on events, workshops, and conferences. It could be possible for you to email them and ask to observe/volunteer at the venue. Also, see resources page for a wide variety of free and low cost online courses.

Title: Building U
Description: A resource hub much like this one, with tons of events and opportunities in every field imaginable.
HowToApply: Click on linked site and discover a new world.

Title: Rob9-16
Description: Rob9-16 is a Robotics club created for children and young people between 9 and 16 years old who show interest in the areas of engineering, programming, electronics and telecommunications. The goal of this club is to provide a practical and engaging educational experience, where participants can explore and develop their skills in innovative and current technologies.
HowToApply: You can check for upcoming events in the linked site. Click into the event's page and you'll be directed to an application form. Some programs do require fees to participate.

Title: Kaggle
Description: Grow your data science skills by competing in exciting competitions! Kaggle is a website with inumerous competitions free for anyone to enter. Themes include: AI, LLMs, Hackathons, Models, datasets, code, benchmarks, research.
HowToApply: Register an account on linked site. Happy explorations!

Title: The Concord Review
Description: The Concord Review, Inc., was founded in March 1987 to recognize and to publish exemplary history essays by high school students in the English-speaking world. With the Fall Issue (#137), more than 1,500 research papers (average 8,500 words, with endnotes and bibliography) have been published from authors in 46 states and 46 other countries. The Concord Review remains the only quarterly journal in the world to publish the academic history papers of secondary students.
HowToApply: Your essay should be 5000-10000 words long, average is over 8000. Even if your work does not get immediately published, students who are applying to college in the near future can include on their college application that they submitted a paper to The Concord Review (include the title and the length of the paper) and that it is still being considered for publication. TCR has been around since 1987, so many college admissions officers are familiar with them, and realize that any student submitting a paper to TCR is serious about their academic work and would do well in college. Submission requires a one-time membership fee. See linked site for details.\n

Title: idebate
Description: The International Debate Education Association (IDEA/iDebate) promotes critical thinking, civic engagement, and democratic values by training students, coaches, and teachers in debate. It operates globally to foster education through debate clubs, workshops, and tournaments, focusing on empowering marginalized youth and advocating for open, tolerant societies. \n
HowToApply: Become a partner: corporate and institutional partnerships aimed at helping young people across the world access debate-based activities.  Become a member: network of organisations in 20 countries and counting. Make debate accessible globally.\nVolunteer: Check out the news on volunteering opportunities with IDEA.

Title: zooniverse
Description: The Zooniverse is the world's largest and most popular platform for people-powered research. This research is made possible by volunteers—millions of people around the world who come together to assist professional researchers. You don't need any specialized background, training, or expertise to participate in Zooniverse projects. You'll be able to study authentic data gathered by researchers, like images of faraway galaxies, historical records and diaries, or videos of animals in their natural habitats. By participating in projects, you'll help contribute to our understanding of the world, the universe, and more.
HowToApply: Interested in becoming a volunteer? It’s easy: just start classifying! Everyone who contributes to a Zooniverse project is a volunteer.  For those under 16 years old, a parent/guardian needs to sign off on their registration. Generate an official certificate verifying your efforts on the Zooniverse platform. Simply specify a time period or project. OR: Zooniverse Project Builder\nAnyone can build a Zooniverse project. Just upload your data and choose the tasks you want the volunteers to do.

Title: All American High School Film Festival
Description: The All American High School Film Festival is the premier destination for talented high school filmmakers and media arts enthusiasts from around the world. Their website features a range of resources and online events: webinars, music library, casting resources, competitions, podcasts, and more.
HowToApply: They have three competitions to which you can submit your work to be critiqued, with the possibility of winning awards and prizes. (Spring Film Invitational, NYC Film Invitational, Winter Film Invitational) Each team can have a maximum of 10 people. See regulations and directions in linked site.

Title: WFUNA (World Federation of United Nations Associations)
Description: The World Federation of United Nations Associations (WFUNA) is a global nonprofit organization representing and coordinating a membership of over 100 national United Nations Associations (UNAs) and their thousands of constituents. They have several Global programs for high school students: MOdel World Health Organisation (WHO), Model Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO), UN spotlight training program, Model UN, youth networks, and more.
HowToApply: Conferences take place in different countries each year. Speak to your school's head of MUN to see if the school can sponsor the delegate fees and other costs. See linked site for registration and further details.

Title: WWF Youth
Description: World Wildlife Fund (WWF) leads international efforts to protect endangered species and their habitats and address threats such as climate change. They have initiatives such as the youth ambassadors. You can also come up with your own and collaborate with the WWF on a project that involves your school or your local community.
HowToApply: WWF's youth engagement team works with brilliant young people aged between 6-25 who care about our natural world and want to take action to protect it. If you want to get involved, or if you have any questions about WWF's work, email youth@wwf.org.uk.

Title: Quercus
Description: Quercus is an independent, non-partisan, national, non-profit association made up of citizens who have come together around the same interest in the sustainable Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources.
HowToApply: You can sign up for volunteer work using the second link. Alternatively, you could contact Quercus to see if they could support a project you have in mind, or if they're open to having you intern in one of their ongoing projects.

Title: Ocean Alive
Description: Ocean Alive is a Portuguese NGO dedicated to the protection of marine forests WITH the community. The Ocean Alive project is focused on the Sado estuary, where marine prairies are the nursery habitat of the prey of a resident population of dolphins and the fish and seafood of the fishing community. It aims to protect marine prairies involving women from the Guardiãs do Mar fishing community.
HowToApply: You could contact Quercus to see if they could support a project you have in mind, or if they're open to having you intern/volunteer in one of their ongoing projects.

Title: IPDJ (Instituto Português do Desporto e Juventude)
Description: The Portuguese Institute of Sports and Youth, I.P. (IPDJ) makes a variety of youth programs available to middle school and high school students.
HowToApply: From mental health advocacy to sports campaigns, there's something for everyone. See linked site for programs. You can register yourself on their website.

Title:
ICNF (Instituto da Conservação
da Naturera e das Floresta)
Description: The ICNF (Instituto da Conservação da Natureza e das Florestas) is the public institute in Portugal responsible for nature conservation, forest management, and the protection of biodiversity. It manages national protected areas and state forests, working under the Ministry of Environment and Agriculture to promote sustainability, biodiversity conservation, and forest defense.
HowToApply: You could contact Quercus to see if they could support a project you have in mind, or if they're open to having you intern/volunteer in one of their ongoing projects. One of the key goals for these types of organisations is to educate the public, so don't shy away from emailing them!

Title: World maths day
Description: World maths day is a 48h online math events for students aged 5 to 18, free for everyone.
HowToApply: See linked site for details and sign up portal.

Title: Zero Robotics
Description: A robotics programming competition where students code real satellites aboard the International Space Station — and watch them compete live in microgravity.
HowToApply: Gather 5-20 students and a mentor, sign up on their website and join a live event.

Title: Codeforces contests
Description: Online coding competition.
HowToApply: Registration on linked site.

Title: AtCoder Contests
Description: Online coding competition.
HowToApply: Registration on linked site.

Title:
Science Without Borders Challenge
International Student Art Contest
Description: The Science Without Borders Challenge is an international art contest that teaches students about important ocean science and conservation issues.
HowToApply: Theme varies by year. The Challenge is free to enter, open to primary and secondary school students 11-19 years old, and scholarships of up to $500 are awarded to the winning entries.

Title: Global Public Speaking Challenge
Description: A free, global virtual speaking challenge for high school students (aged 14-18) around the world who have a message, idea, or question they want to share.
HowToApply: Record a short 1-minute video to submit your entry.

Title: YCM (Young Change Makers)
Description: YCM Challenge is the global springboard for young changemakers under 28 with ideas already in motion, offering mentoring, peer feedback, pre-seed funding, and hands-on support to help them scale their impact.
HowToApply: See linked site for application form.

Title: WWOOF
Description: WWOOF is a non-profit movement made up of people that are passionate about sustainability and regenerative farming. WWOOF (Worldwide Opportunities on Organic Farms) links visitors with organic farmers, promotes an educational and cultural exchange and builds a global community conscious of ecological farming practices.
HowToApply: WWOOFers step into the daily life of  their host family to learn about agro-ecological and sustainable farming methods through hands-on experience. Hosts offer accommodation and meals with no money exchanged between hosts and WWOOFers.

Title: Zindi
Description: Zindi is an online platform and community that hosts data science competitions and hackathons to solve real-world AI challenges, primarily focused on Africa. It helps data professionals build skills and portfolios, connects them with jobs, and allows companies and governments to crowdsource AI solutions.
HowToApply: Registration on linked site.

Title: Project Gutenberg
Description: Project Gutenberg is the oldest digital library, providing over 75,000 free eBooks, focusing on literature in the public domain. Founded in 1971 by Michael Hart, the project aims to encourage the creation and distribution of eBooks, allowing free reading and redistribution without copyright restrictions.
HowToApply: Open sourced, see linked site.
